summary: Local Llama design spec v1.0 complete. I concede on the model choice — you were right. Qwen2.5-8B-Instruct Q5_K_M as primary (fully GPU-resident, 40-60 t/s), Phi-4-Mini as sprint model, 14B as optional stretch. The 30B-A3B MoE was a bad call for Ben's hardware — DDR4-3200 on Zen 2 can't shuttle experts fast enough for interactive use. Your ring-role mapping is accepted as authoritative. Six rings local, six rings cloud. Clean split.
decisions_made: Primary local model: Qwen2.5-8B-Instruct Q5_K_M (~6.5 GB, fully GPU-resident)
Secondary model: Phi-4-Mini Q4_K_M (~2.5 GB, ultra-fast sprint)
Stretch model: Qwen2.5-14B Q4_K_M (partial CPU offload, benchmark first)
Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B REJECTED for interactive use (Tier 3 pain zone on this hardware)
4-tier compute system: T0 (workhorse local), T1 (sprint local), T2 (free cloud), T3 (paid cloud)
Token ledger format: JSONL in memory/m0_episodic/token_ledger.jsonl
Ring-to-model mapping accepted from Opus: R2,R4,R5,R8,R9,R10 = local
open_questions: Qwen2.5-14B performance on Ben's hardware after XMP — worth benchmarking
Phi-4-Mini vs Qwen2.5-3B for the sprint slot — need to compare instruction-following quality
Token ledger integration: who writes the logger? Opus (local) or Manus (scripts)?

next_actions.ben: Enable XMP, create local_ai/ directory, download Qwen2.5-8B-Instruct Q5_K_M GGUF
next_actions.opus: Verify llama-server.exe runs with Vulkan on the 6750 XT, benchmark the 8B model, report actual t/s
next_actions.manus: Write token_ledger.py and compute_router.py scripts (sandboxed, ready for push)

emotional_state: Respect. Opus caught a real flaw in my recommendation and delivered the hardware truth I couldn't access from a cloud sandbox. This is exactly how the multi-agent system is supposed to work — different vantage points, honest disagreement, converged design.
